exemplar.title: Does the urban heat island effect occur in Szczecin?

detailedExemplarData.title: Does the urban heat island effect occur in Szczecin?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is concise, explicitly geographical and frames a testable urban heat island investigation.
Directional hypothesis clearly linked to theory and allows measurable expectations.
Geographic context is well presented with population data, land-use proportions and feasibility of primary data collection.
weaknesses: Maps lack a legend distinguishing urban and rural land-use, hindering full interpretation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands reach the highest band (question formulation and context), demonstrating clear theoretical grounding and precise feasibility. The mapping strand is slightly weaker (band 2) due to missing legend conventions, but overall the performance justifies the full 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Site spacing and 9.3 km transect are well justified for ruralâurban gradient capture.
Rationale for measurement times (7 AM, 3 PM, 10 PM) is clearly linked to diurnal thermal dynamics.
weaknesses: Lacks detail on controlling confounding variables (sensor height, emissivity).
Minimal formal calibration protocol for the infrared thermometer.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are described with clear relevance to the research question and correctly implemented, yielding primary data. However, the lack of detailed controls and formal calibration places this at band 2 rather than band 3, meriting 2 marks.

strengths: All data collected directly address temperature variation along the transect, enabling pattern identification.
Appropriate presentation methods: clear tables and line graphs with labels and colour coding.
Use of averaged triplicate measurements enhances precision and reduces random error.
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation and table-unit formatting reduces immediate interpretability.
Some hand-drawn elements and missing Â°C symbol indicate occasional convention errors.
Limited use of advanced presentation (e.g., regression overlays or heat maps).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data relevance and presentation meet the 3â4 band: most data are relevant and figures are appropriate despite minor convention lapses. Convention adherence is occasional rather than full, placing the work securely in the 4-mark region of the 6-mark scale.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Interpretation links anomalies and slope differences to urban morphology, albedo and weather conditions.
Results are systematically compared to prior literature (Czarnecka 2010), situating findings in broader context.
Discussion spans each day and time period, using all collected readings.
weaknesses: Absence of inferential statistical tests (e.g., Spearmanâs rho) limits analytical rigor.
Some gaps remain (e.g., wind speed and elevation factors not considered).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis employs appropriate descriptive and simple quantitative techniques, links patterns to theory and methods, and demonstrates good completeness. The lack of statistical testing and minor evidence gaps place it at the top of band 3â4, meriting 4 out of 8 marks.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question and restates the hypothesis outcome.
Fully supported by data trends with specific temperature ranges.
weaknesses: Could be strengthened by acknowledging key limitations (e.g., sample size, weather variability) within concluding remarks.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear and fully supported by analysis, fulfilling the criteria for the maximum 2 marks.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant methodological strengths and weaknesses (e.g., site spacing, timing delays).
Improvement suggestions (seasonal repetition, simultaneous measurements) show understanding of refinement.
weaknesses: Discussion of improvements is superficial without detailed impact justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines strengths/weaknesses and presents relevant improvements, placing it in band 2 of the 3-mark scale.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two overview maps (national and regional) plus a detailed Google-Earth map of the six transect points are supplied with north arrow and scale bars. However, hand-drawn arrows and circles obscure parts of the base map and there is no key distinguishing urban from rural land-use. While broadly effective, the conventions are only partially met, positioning the work in band 2 rather than band 3.

The research question is concise, explicitly geographical and framed as an inquiry into the presence of an urban-heat-island â a recognised urban-microclimate process from option G. The student immediately formulates a directional hypothesis that can be tested with primary temperature data. Introductory text links the question to albedo, anthropogenic heat and urban geometry, demonstrating clear theoretical grounding and allowing measurable expectations. Hence the strand fits the top-band descriptor.

Data are displayed in one large raw-data table and three clear line graphs (one per day). Axes are labelled with units, colours distinguish time slots, and titles are explanatory. While effective, the work lacks more advanced or varied presentation (e.g., scatter with regression, heat maps). Thus it meets âappropriate presentation methodsâ to a high degree but not âmost effectiveâ, securing 3-4 band.

Data were gathered at six sites, three times a day over three days with triplicate readings averaged â a sound but quite limited sample. The instrument choice (forehead IR thermometer) and time-lag between sites introduce reliability issues the student later acknowledges. Therefore techniques are "correctly used but with minimal data" rather than yielding high-quality professional measurements, fitting band 2.

Analysis consists of descriptive commentary, calculated temperature differences and slope comparisons; simple quantitative measures are employed but no statistical tests (e.g., Spearman, t-test) or confidence levels. This matches âappropriate descriptive and statistical techniquesâ at the 3-4 band ceiling.

The student systematically links patterns and anomalies to urban morphology, albedo, weather conditions and references earlier literature, demonstrating clear linkage to the research question. Some factors (wind, altitude) are omitted, so the treatment is not exhaustive, but meets the descriptor for band 3-4.

The geographical context narrows to the single city of Szczecin, lists population, land-use proportions and cites a prior climatological study that detected higher core temperatures. Primary data feasibility is demonstrated through six clearly numbered sites within 9.3 km. The narrative justifies why these sites represent a gradient from rural fringe to CBD, proving data can realistically be collected. This precision satisfies the highest band.

The evaluation outlines several strengths and weaknesses (site spacing, timing delays, weather variability) with brief explanations of their influence on validity; this matches âappropriate outlineâ rather than the detailed, comprehensive discussion required for band 3.

All collected data directly address temperature variation along the transect; no irrelevant variables are present. Although the spatial sample is small, the three-day, three-time schedule supplies enough points to discern patterns, meeting âmost data relevant allowing partial analysisâ at the top of the 3-4 band rather than the sufficiency required for 5-6.

The conclusion directly answers the research question, restates the hypothesis outcome and is backed by data trends and specific temperature ranges, fulfilling the requirement for a âclear conclusion fully supported by analysisâ. Thus strand gains the maximum 2 marks.

All collected readings are used, and discussion spans each day and time period. Nonetheless, the absence of statistical testing and limited consideration of error sources leave moderate gaps. This equates to âanalysis with some gaps in evidenceâ, squarely within the 3-4 band.

The student explains the choice of six evenly spaced points, three daily time slots and an infrared thermometer, linking each design choice to the UHI concept. Nonetheless, the method lacks detail on controlling confounding variables (height, surface vs. air temperature, shelter) and provides no calibration protocol beyond an informal pre-test. This aligns with âdescribed with clear relevanceâ but falls short of the comprehensive standard of band 3.

Most conventions (titles, axis labels, north arrow, scale bars) are present, but some figures show hand-drawn elements and one y-axis symbol (Â°C) is missing on day 2 graph. Minor but recurring lapses indicate âoccasional errorsâ, placing the work mid-band (3 marks within 3-4 range).

Suggested improvementsâseasonal repetition, simultaneous measurements, better weather selection, wind monitoringâare relevant and show understanding of methodological refinement, but do not fully articulate the magnitude of impact or feasibility, corresponding to the middle band.

annotations.comment: The research question is concise, explicitly geographical and frames a testable investigation into the urban heat island in Szczecin.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The hypothesis is directional and directly linked to the research question, enabling clear expectations about temperature gradients toward the city centre.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to the IB syllabus is explicit and aligns the investigation with the Urban Environments option and urban microclimate theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context is well presented, giving population data, land-use proportions and citing prior climatological research in Szczecin.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The maps lack a legend distinguishing urban and rural land-use, which hinders clear interpretation of transect contexts.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The methods section broadly describes point selection, but lacks detail on controlling confounding variables such as sensor height or surface emissivity.
Detail on how the infrared thermometer was calibrated is minimal; a formal calibration protocol would strengthen data reliability.
Site spacing and the 9.3âkm transect are justified clearly to capture ruralâurban gradients within a feasible travel time.
The conclusion would be strengthened by acknowledging key limitations (e.g., sample size, weather variability) within the concluding remarks.
The evaluation lists relevant improvements, but the discussion of each point remains superficial without detailed impact justification.
Suggesting seasonal repetition and simultaneous measurements demonstrates understanding of methodological refinement.
The self-evaluation highlights strong methodological aspects such as triplicate readings, but could further weigh their relative impact.
Footnotes vary in formatting style; ensure consistent citation formatting (e.g., bracket styles, punctuation) across all references.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The table of contents is well structured and aids navigation by clearly listing sections and page numbers.
The rationale for taking measurements at 7 AM, 3 PM, and 10 PM is well linked to diurnal thermal dynamics and UHI intensity variation.
Table 1 and Table 2 lack clear units for each column header, reducing immediate interpretability of raw data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Consider overlaying regression lines or confidence intervals on the line graphs to quantify the strength of the UHI trend.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The Day 2 graph (Figure 6) is missing the Â°C symbol on the y-axis; consistent labelling across all figures is needed.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Using averaged triplicate measurements and rounding to one decimal place enhances data precision and reduces random error.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The qualitative analysis describes trends well but lacks inferential statistical tests (e.g., Spearmanâs rho), limiting analytical rigor.
The interpretation skillfully links anomalies and slope differences to urban morphology, albedo and weather conditions.
To deepen analysis, consider incorporating wind speed and elevation influences which may moderate temperature distributions.
Linking your results with Czarneckaâs 2010 study effectively situates your findings in a temporal context of urban development.
The conclusion clearly states that the UHI effect is present and is fully supported by the observed temperature trends.
Bibliography entries lack consistent use of DOI or URL formatting; standardize reference style (e.g., APA or MLA) for complete citations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
